predecessor_carry_forward.rationale_quoted: Ruby backtick subshell `\`#{cmd}\`` parses as `subshell` node, not `call_expression`. AST shape inexpressible without FP risk in current call_node_kinds(Ruby). References FAI-v1 `\bgets\b` precedent verbatim — same shape of carry-forward (raw-substring/AST-node mismatch documented as out-of-scope).

fixture.fp_critical_observation: FP fixture mentions the WORD 'backtick cmd' inside a string literal but DOES NOT contain a real `…` subshell node. Therefore an AST dispatch keyed on the `subshell` node-kind cannot regress this FP.

test_runner.assertion: expected ≥1 command_injection finding for fixture ruby/command_injection_positive.rb (lang=ruby)

supertypes_present_in: _arg (named: true) — subshell appears as a subtype of _arg
_primary (named: true) — subshell appears as a subtype of _primary

tree_sitter_ruby_node_audit.interpolation_node: interpolation contains an _expression child (e.g., identifier `cmd` for `#{cmd}`)
tree_sitter_ruby_node_audit.interpolation_subtree_strings: Interpolation children include `string_content` and `escape_sequence` for non-tainted byte ranges. Inner expression is a regular identifier subtree.

percent_x_form.verification: tree-sitter-ruby 0.23.1 parses `%x{ls}` as a `subshell` node (same node kind as backtick form). Confirmed by reading node-types.json: there is no separate %x node kind — the grammar collapses both lexical forms (`…` and %x{…}/%x[…]/%x(…)) onto the single `subshell` named-node type.
percent_x_form.implication: ruby-backtick-extraction-v1 closes both `\`cmd\`` and `%x{cmd}` shapes in a single dispatch addition. The fixture exercises only the backtick form; we may add a `%x{...}` regression-guard fixture in M2 to lock the extra coverage in.

is_in_string_filter_safety.verdict: subshell is NOT a string kind. is_in_string walking up from the subshell node returns false. The interpolation's inner identifier (`cmd`) walks up: identifier → interpolation → subshell → method (block) → … — none are `string`/`string_content`. The descendants-loop's `is_in_string` skip will NOT filter out a subshell node nor an inner identifier inside `#{...}`.

dispatch_audit.subshell_extract_call_name_today: Returns None — `subshell` is NOT in the match arms.
dispatch_audit.consequence_of_option_a_alone: Adding `subshell` to call_node_kinds(Ruby) ALONE is insufficient. Both detect_sinks_ast (taint.rs:4276-4282) and the W2-pre call-shape path in member_patterns_match (taint.rs:3886-3906) gate on `extract_call_name(...)` returning Some(_). With `subshell` absent from extract_call_name_ruby's match arms, both paths early-terminate and no sink fires.
member_patterns_match_paths: L3853-3870: structural field-access match — does NOT apply to subshell (no member_access_expression around it)
L3886-3906: W2-pre call-shape path — extract_call_name returns None → no match

L3913-3917: raw-substring fallback — pat_rcv must be empty AND descendant_text must contain pat_field. The descendant_text for a subshell node is the FULL backtick text `\`cmd\``. Adding ('', '`') as a pattern would match but would also match any line that has a backtick CHARACTER anywhere — high FP class. NOT recommended.

options_considered.rejected_reason: Insufficient alone — extract_call_name_ruby would also need a new arm. Even with both edits, side-effect risk: `subshell` would be classified as a 'call' for ALL passing through call_node_kinds, including detect_sources_ast (would never fire because RUBY_AST_SOURCES has no call_names matching subshell text), the W2-pre call-shape path in member_patterns_match (could mistakenly match member_patterns against dotted call-name parsing of synthetic subshell text), find_parent_assignment_var, references.rs:3325 is_call check (consumed by reference-classification — would mark subshells as calls in places they aren't). Risk surface broader than the value.

options_considered.summary: Add 'subshell' to call_node_kinds(Ruby) AND extend extract_call_name_ruby to return synthetic name (e.g., 'subshell' or '`')
options_considered.rejected_reason: Same broad side-effect surface as Option A. Synthetic call name pollutes downstream consumers (rr_baseline_per_language_test, references.rs is_call gate). Touches ast_utils.rs (cross-cutting helper) when a localized change in taint.rs would suffice.

options_considered.summary: Add a dedicated dispatch arm in detect_sinks_ast (taint.rs:4256-4360) that fires when language == Ruby AND descendant.kind() == 'subshell', producing a synthetic ShellExec sink with var extracted via the existing extract_first_identifier_arg_ast helper (it descends through interpolation children to find the inner identifier).
options_considered.rationale: Localized to taint.rs (no ast_utils.rs cross-cutting change). No change to call_node_kinds → no side-effect on source detection, sanitizer detection, references.rs, or rr_baseline tests. extract_first_identifier_arg_ast's existing BFS over named children already descends through `interpolation` to reach the inner identifier (verified by reading taint.rs:3934-3982 generic path and the PHP arms at 3948-3983 — the generic body walks descendants for an identifier). LOC delta ~10-20.

design_decision.selected_summary: Dedicated subshell dispatch arm in detect_sinks_ast (taint.rs around L4256-L4360). Treats Ruby `subshell` nodes as ShellExec sinks; var-extraction reuses extract_first_identifier_arg_ast's generic descend-and-find-identifier path.

extract_first_identifier_arg_ast_audit.verdict_for_subshell: GENERIC PATH (post all the language-specific shortcut arms) walks named descendants of the input node looking for an identifier. For a subshell containing `#{cmd}`, the descendants are: subshell → interpolation → identifier(cmd). The generic body's `walk_descendants` or BFS scan picks up the identifier. Verified by reading the body — for non-PHP / non-OCaml-specific input, the function descends arguments-list children OR scans for the first identifier child of the input node.
extract_first_identifier_arg_ast_audit.fallback_if_helper_returns_none: If extract_first_identifier_arg_ast cannot find an identifier (e.g., %x{ls} with no interpolation), the dispatch arm should still emit a TaintSink with a synthetic var (e.g., '__subshell__' or the leading-identifier-of-the-line) — mirroring the M5-carry-forward synthetic-var pattern used for Elixir System.cmd([]) and OCaml Sqlite3.exec multi-receiver shapes. Decision: return None → no sink emitted (better — the test fixture has an interpolation, so identifier extraction WILL succeed). For %x{} without interpolation we accept zero-detection (a pure-static `%x{ls}` is not a tainted-flow case anyway).

side_effect_audit.sources: RUBY_AST_SOURCES (taint.rs:2740-2756) has 3 STDIN call_names + 2 File member_patterns + ('', 'params[') + ('', 'ENV['). NONE of these would fire on a subshell node — call_names paths require descendant.kind() ∈ call_node_kinds (we are NOT changing it); member_patterns_match raw-fallback for ('', 'params[') against subshell text could falsely match if a subshell's interpolated text contained 'params['. Defensive note: the canonical fixture's subshell is `\`#{cmd}\``, NOT `\`#{params[:cmd]}\``, so the practical FP class is empty. The FP-shape (params[ inside a subshell) would be caught by raw-fallback regardless of this milestone — out of scope.
side_effect_audit.sinks: Ruby-specific dispatch path is NEW (Option B). Affects ONLY descendants whose kind == 'subshell' AND language == Ruby. No other AST sink pattern is keyed on subshell.
side_effect_audit.sanitizers: RUBY_AST_SANITIZERS has no entries that depend on subshell. Sanitizers also gate on call_node_kinds, so they are unaffected.
side_effect_audit.references_rs: references.rs:3325 (is_call check) consumes call_node_kinds. Unchanged → no impact.
side_effect_audit.rr_baseline_per_language_test: rr_baseline_per_language_test.rs:276 references RUBY_AST_SINKS `system` call_name. Unchanged. Adding the subshell dispatch arm does not modify any AST bank's call_names, so this test is unaffected.
side_effect_audit.fp_regression_guard: command_injection_string_literal_fp.rb mentions 'backtick' inside a string literal but contains NO real subshell. The descendants-walk over its parsed AST yields zero `subshell` nodes → zero FPs from this milestone.

loc_estimate.breakdown: +~12 LOC for the dispatch arm in detect_sinks_ast + ~3 LOC explanatory comment. The build_sinks_ast_index (per-line worklist consumer) at taint.rs:~3886 mirrors the same loop and may need the same arm — verify in M1. If both loops need the arm, +~24 LOC total (still within bound).

tigers: T1: Adding subshell to call_node_kinds(Ruby) without extending extract_call_name_ruby → silent no-op (already considered, rejected via Option B).

T2: extract_first_identifier_arg_ast generic body fails to descend through `interpolation` named-child to reach the inner identifier — fixture stays RED. M1 verifies empirically with cargo test pre-M2; if it fails, M2 extends the helper to add a Ruby-specific subshell descent arm (still localized to taint.rs).
T3: A second dispatch loop (build_sinks_ast_index or similar) is missed → fixture transitions GREEN under cargo test but stays RED under tldr vuln binary smoke. M3 includes binary-smoke verification.
elephants: E1: %x{cmd} regression — milestone adds a fixture in M2 to lock the extra coverage in.
E2: Heredoc + backtick combination — out of scope (no carry-forward test asks for it).
